response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I spend hours every day scrolling through social media and I have to tell you, the amount of complete garbage people share without thinking drives me absolutely crazy. As someone who works with my hands building real things that actually have to stand up and be solid, I cannot stand when people just throw around fake information like it doesn't matter. When I build a deck or frame a house, everything has to be square and true or the whole thing falls apart. Same should go for what people post online, but it doesn't. I see people in my feed sharing the most ridiculous stories about everything from politics to health remedies to supposedly true crime stories that are completely made up. What really gets under my skin is when my own friends and family share this stuff without even checking if it's real. I'll call them out on it too, because somebody has to. I don't care if it makes me unpopular. The way I deal with fake content is pretty straightforward. First, if something seems too crazy or too perfect, it probably is. I've learned to look at who's posting it and whether they have any actual credibility. Second, I'll do a quick search to see if real news sources are covering it. If only random websites with weird names are talking about it, that's a red flag. Third, I'm not afraid to tell people they're wrong. If someone posts fake news, I'll comment and let them know. Some people get mad at me for it, but honestly I don't really care about hurting feelings when the truth matters more. The problem is that social media companies don't do nearly enough to stop this stuff from spreading. They care more about engagement and advertising money than actually protecting people from lies. It stresses me out constantly seeing how much misinformation is out there and how easily people believe it. I wish there were stricter rules and consequences for spreading fake content, but until then, we all have to be more skeptical and willing to speak up when we see something that's clearly false.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 46-year-old self-employed carpenter with lower secondary education who spends several hours daily on social media. Key traits informing the response: High extraversion (72) suggests they're outspoken and socially engaged online. Extremely low agreeableness (2) means they're confrontational, blunt, and willing to challenge others without concern for social harmony. Very high neuroticism (100) indicates they experience significant stress and anxiety about issues like misinformation. Low openness (25) suggests they prefer straightforward, practical approaches and traditional thinking. Their carpentry background provides a concrete, hands-on framework for understanding truth and accuracy. Moderate conscientiousness (48) means they make some effort to verify information but aren't extremely systematic. Their Christian beliefs and several hours of daily social media use mean they're exposed to and concerned about fake content. The tone is direct, somewhat aggressive, stress-laden, and unapologetic, reflecting their personality traits.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, confrontational, stressed, blunt, and unapologetic with practical analogies from carpentry work
